songs.title: All Through The Night
songs.tab_content:
----------------------------------------------------------------------
Song name: All Through The Night  by  Misc Traditional
----------------------------------------------------------------------
Traditional late 18th century Welsh folk song. Several lyric versions out there so apologies
to any Welsh folk if I have used the 'wrong' lyrics!
In the first 2 lines of the bridges it is possible to omit the C chords and just play the
F, Dm, Am and G and it still sounds ok imho.

Intro = C F D G F G C

Verse 1
C        F         D       G
Sleep my child and peace attend thee
F   G           C 
All through the night 
C        F      D        G
Guardian angels God will send thee
F   G           C 
All through the night

Bridge 1
F    C   F   C  F     C   F   C
Soft the drowsy hours are creeping
F    C   Dm   C  Am  C   G  
Hill and vale in slumber sleeping
C    F      D     G 
I my loving vigil keeping 
F   G           C
All through the night

Verse 2
C         F        D        G
While the moon her watch is keeping
F   G           C 
All through the night 
C         F     D        G        
While the weary world is sleeping
F   G           C 
All through the night

Bridge 2
F    C   F   C  F   C  F   C
O'er thy spirit gently stealing
F  C    Dm C Am    C G 
Visions of delight revealing 
C          F        D    G
Breathes a pure and holy feeling 
F   G           C
All through the night

Verse 3
C       F       D            G
Love to thee my thoughts are turning 
F   G           C
All through the night 
C       F       D        G
All for thee my heart is yearning
F   G           C 
All through the night

Bridge 3
F      C   F    C   F     C   F C
Though sad fate our lives may sever
F  C    Dm   C   Am   C G  
Parting will not last forever
C         F         D         G
There's a hope that leaves me never
F   G           C 
All through the night

songs.title: Amazing Grace
songs.tab_content:
----------------------------------------------------------------------
Song name: Amazing Grace  by  Misc Praise Songs
----------------------------------------------------------------------
    G7
e|-----|
B|--3--|
G|--4--|
D|--3--|
A|-----|
E|--3--|


[Refrain]
 G      G7         C         G
Amazing Grace, how sweet the sound
             A           D
That saved a wretch like me!
  G        G7        C      G
I once was lost, but now am found,
    Em         D     G
Was blind, but now I see.


[Verse 1]
      G          G7        C        G
'Twas grace that taught my heart to fear,
             A       D
And grace my fears relieved.
    G        G7       C      G
How precious did that grace appear,
    Em     D       G
The hour I first believed.


[Verse 2]
      G      G7       C          G
Through many dangers, toils and snares
           A    D
We have already come
       G         G7         C          G
'Twas grace hath brought us safe thus far
     Em        D       G
And grace will lead us home


[Verse 3]
    G        G7       C       G
The Lord has promised good to me,
            A      D
His word my hope secures;
   G       G7         C       G
He will my shield and portion be
   Em      D      G
as long as life endures.


[Verse 4]
     G          G7        C        G
When we've been there ten thousand years,
               A      D
Bright shining as the sun,
      G       G7      C          G
We've no less days to sing God's praise
     Em        D       G
Than when we'd first begun.


[Refrain]
 G      G7         C         G
Amazing Grace, how sweet the sound
             A           D
That saved a wretch like me!
  G        G7        C      G
I once was lost, but now am found,
    Em         D     G
Was blind, but now I see.

songs.title: Arthur Mcbride
songs.tab_content:
----------------------------------------------------------------------
Song name: Arthur Mcbride  by  Paul Brady
----------------------------------------------------------------------
G Am7 G/B C walkup can be played like

e|-------------|
B|-0-1-3-5-----|
G|-0-0-0-0-----|
D|-------------|
A|---0-2-3-----|
E|-3-----------|


[Intro]
G
G    Am7 G/B C G    Cadd9
G                   D7
G    Am7 G/B C G    Cadd9
G                   D7
G


[Verse 1]
    G         Am7        G              Am7
Oh, me and my cousin one Arthur McBride
G/B C        G/B          C
As we went a-walking down by the seaside
    G/B       Am7          G
Now mark what followed and what did betide
       G/B      G         D7
For it being on Christmas morning
    G/B      Am7      G
And for recreation we went on a tramp
Am7 G/B C           G/B         C
And we met Sergeant Napper and Corporal Vamp
        G/B        Am7        G
And the little wee drummer intending to camp
        G/B       G            D7   G
For the day being pleasant and charming


[Verse 2]
      G             Am7          G/B
“Good morning, good morning” the Sergeant did cry
 Am7 G/B C           G/B         C
“And the same to you gentlemen,” we did reply
  G/B        Am7        G
Intending no harm as we meant to pass by
       G/B      G         D7
For it being on Christmas morning
         G/B         Am7        G
But says he “My fine fellows if you will enlist
Am7  G/B C          G/B         C
It’s ten guineas in gold I will slip in your fists
      G/B          Am7            G
And a crown in the bargain for to kick up the dust
    G/B              G             D7  G   D7
And drink the King’s health in the morning.


[Verse 3]
      G/B        Am7     G
For a soldier he leads a very fine life
Am7 G/B C         G/B            C
And he  always is blessed with a charming young wife,
       G/B          Am7           G
And he pays all his debts without sorrow and strife
    G/B          G            D7
And always lives pleasant and charming.
      G/B        Am7       G
And a soldier he always is decent and clean
Am7 G/B C         G/B           C
 In the finest of clothing he’s constantly seen
      G/B        Am7          G
While other poor fellows look dirty and mean
    G/B         G            D7  G
And sup on thin gruel in the morning.”


[Instrumental]
G    Am7 G/B C G    Cadd9
G                   D7
G    Am7 G/B C G    Cadd9
G                   D7
G


[Verse 4]
     G          Am7         G/B           G
Says Arthur, “I wouldn’t be proud of your clothes
Am7 G/B    C        G/B           C
For you’ve only the lend of them, as I suppose,
        G/B      Am7             G
And you dare not change them one night for you know
Am7 G/B C            G/B            D7
If  you do you’ll be flogged in the morning.
      G/B         Am7    G               Am7
And although that we are single and free,
G/B C           G/B          C
We take great delight in our own company
       G/B       Am7          G
And we have no desire strange faces to see
  G/B              G          D7  G    D7
Although that your offers are charming.


[Verse 5]
       G/B       Am7     G                 Am7
And we have no desire to take your advance,
G/B C           G/B        C
All hazards and dangers we barter on chance.
        G/B           Am7             G
For you would have no scruples for to send us to France
      G/B          G            D7  G
Where we would get shot without warning.”


[Verse 6]
    G             Am7             G/B          G
“Oh no,” says the Sergeant, “I’ll have no such chat
Am7 G/B C            G/B          C
And I   neither will take it from spalpeen or brat
    G/B      Am7          G
For if you insult me with one other word
     G/B          G            D7
I’ll cut off your heads in the morning.”
         G/B        Am7  G
And then Arthur and I we soon drew our odds
Am7 G/B C                G/B         C
And we  scarce gave them time for to draw their own blades
       G/B        Am7         G
When a trusty shillelagh came over their heads
    G/B            G            D7  G   D7
And bade them take that as fair warning


[Verse 7]
          G/B       Am7          G                  Am7
And their old rusty rapiers that hung by their sides
G/B C             G/B       C
We  flung them as far as we could in the tide
     G/B            Am7            G
“Now take them out, devils,” cried Arthur McBride
     G/B          G           D7
“And temper their edge in the morning.”
        G/B        Am7        G
And the little wee drummer we flattened his pow
Am7 G/B C          G/B         C
And we  made a football of his rowdy dow dow
G/B             Am7         G
Threw it in the tide for to rock and to roll
    G/B       G         D7  G   D7
And bade it a tedious returning


[Verse 8]
       G/B       Am7         G
And we having no money, paid them off in cracks
Am G/B C       G/B              C
And we paid no respect to their two bloody backs
       G/B           Am7          G
But we lathered them there like a pair of wet sacks
    G/B           G           D7
And left them for dead in the morning
    G/B      Am7          G               Am7
And so to conclude and to finish disputes
 G/B C        G/B           C
We obligingly asked if they wanted recruits
    G/B         Am7            G
For we were the lads who would give them hard clouts
    G/B           G            D7  G
And bid them look sharp in the morning


[Instrumental]
G    Am7 G/B C G    Cadd9
G                   D7
G


[Verse 9]
    G/B       Am7        G              Am7
Oh, me and my cousin one Arthur McBride
G/B C         G/B          C
As  we went a-walking down by the seaside
    G/B       Am7          G
Now mark what followed and what did betide
       G/B      G         D7  G       G
For it being on Christmas morning

songs.title: Auld Lang Syne
songs.tab_content:
----------------------------------------------------------------------
Song name: Auld Lang Syne  by  Misc Traditional
----------------------------------------------------------------------
Auld Lang Syne
Words by Robert Burns

Best song to ever ring in the New Year. This may not be completely correct, but
it sounds pretty good to my ears. Happy holidays, and enjoy!

[Verse 1]
        G                D
Should auld acquaintance be forgot,
    Em               C
And never brought to mind?
       G                 D
Should auld acquaintance be forgot,
    Em   C    G
And auld lang syne!

[Chorus]
    G         D
For auld lang syne, my dear,
    G         C
For auld lang syne.
      G             D
We'll take a cup o' kindness yet,
    Em   C    G
For auld lang syne.

[Verse 2]
      G                D
And surely you’ll buy your pint cup!
     Em              C
And surely I’ll buy mine!
           G             D
And we'll take a cup o’ kindness yet,
     Em  C    G
for auld lang syne.

[Chorus]
    G         D
For auld lang syne, my dear,
    G         C
For auld lang syne.
      G             D
We'll take a cup o' kindness yet,
    Em   C    G
For auld lang syne.

[Verse 3]
   G              D
We two have run about the slopes,
     Em                 C
And picked the daisies fine;
           G              D
But we’ve wandered many a weary foot,
      Em   C    G
Since auld lang syne.

[Chorus]
    G         D
For auld lang syne, my dear,
    G         C
For auld lang syne.
      G             D
We'll take a cup o' kindness yet,
    Em   C    G
For auld lang syne.

[Verse 4]
   G                D
We two have paddled in the stream,
      Em               C
From morning sun till dine;
      G              D
But seas between us broad have roared
      Em   C    G
Since auld lang syne.

[Chorus]
    G         D
For auld lang syne, my dear,
    G         C
For auld lang syne.
      G             D
We'll take a cup o' kindness yet,
    Em   C    G
For auld lang syne.

[Verse 5]
     G                D
And there’s a hand my trusty friend!
    Em                 C
And give me a hand o’ thine!
    G                  D
And we’ll take a right good-will draught,
      Em  C    G
For auld lang syne.

[Chorus]
    G         D
For auld lang syne, my dear,
    G         C
For auld lang syne.
      G             D
We'll take a cup o' kindness yet,
    Em   C    G
For auld lang syne.

songs.title: Banks Of The Roses
songs.tab_content:
----------------------------------------------------------------------
Song name: Banks Of The Roses  by  The Dubliners
----------------------------------------------------------------------
[Chorus]
         C                    G              C
On the banks of the roses my love and I sat down.
    F                C           G              C
And I took out my fiddle for to play my love a tune,
         F            C                  G             Am
In the middle of the tune, me boy, she smiled and she said,
     C              F                G   C
Oh Johnny, lovely Johnny don't you leave me.

     C                    G              C
When I was a young lad I heard me father say,
      F           C                G            C
That he'd rather see me dead,and buried in the clay,
  F               C        G       Am
Sooner than be married to any run away,
         C            F           G C
By the lovely sweet banks of the roses.

[Chorus]
C                    G              C
On the banks of the roses my love and I sat down.
    F                C           G              C
And I took out my fiddle for to play my love a tune,
         F            C                  G             Am
In the middle of the tune, me boy, she smiled and she said,
     C              F                G   C
Oh Johnny, lovely Johnny don't you leave me.


         C                     G                  C
Well now I am no run away and soon I'll have you know,
     F                C                G        C
That I can take the bottle, or I can leave it alone,
             F            C               G                 Am
And if her daddy doesn't like it, he can keep his daughter home,
            C              F          G   C
And young Johnny will go roving with another.

[Chorus]
C                    G              C
On the banks of the roses my love and I sat down.
    F                C           G              C
And I took out my fiddle for to play my love a tune,
         F            C                  G             Am
In the middle of the tune, me boy, she smiled and she said,
     C              F                G   C

C                            G                C

When the leaves they are green, and the meadows they are gay,

And me and my true love will sit and sport and play.
         C            F           G C
By the lovely sweet banks of the roses.

[Chorus]
C                    G              C
On the banks of the roses my love and I sat down.
    F                C           G              C
And I took out my fiddle for to play my love a tune,
         F            C                  G             Am
In the middle of the tune, me boy, she smiled and she said,

     C              F                G   C
Oh Johnny, lovely Johnny don't you leave me.

songs.title: Banks Of The Sweet Primroses
songs.tab_content:
----------------------------------------------------------------------
Song name: Banks Of The Sweet Primroses  by  Fairport Convention
----------------------------------------------------------------------
[Intro]
G   C  D  x4
G

[Verse]
                     C           D
As I walked out on a midsummer's morning
                G             C        D
For to view the fields and to take the air
            G            C         D
Down by the banks of the sweet primroses
          G           C    D   G
There I beheld a most love lie fair

[Verse]
                     C          D
Three short steps, I stepped up to her
            G          C         D
Not knowing her as she passed me by
             G            C          D
I stepped up to her, thinking for to view her
                G            C  D     G
She appeared to be like some virtuous bride

[Verse]
                          C       D
I says, "Fair maid, where are you going
                 G         C        D
And what's the occasion of all your grief?
                   G        C   D
I will make you as happy as any lady
            G               C     D G
If you will grant to me one small relief"

[Instrumental]
G   C  D  x4
G

[Verse]
                                C       D
"Stand off, stand off, you're a false deceiver
          G               C      D
You are a false deceitful man, I know
                  G              C        D
'Tis you that has caused my poor heart to wander
            G       C    D    G
And in your comfort lies no refrain"

[Verse]
                         C        D
"So I'll go down to some lonesome valley
                G           C        D
Where no man on earth shall there me find
                        G              C            D
Where the pretty little small birds do change their voices
          G            C   D      G
And every moment blows blusterous wind"

[Verse]
                          C    D
So come all young men who go a-sailing
           G          C      D
Pray pay attention to what I say
                   G          C      D
For there's many a dark and a cloudy morning
             G    C  D     G
Turns out to be a sunshiny day

[Outro]
G   C D G

songs.title: Barbra Allen
songs.tab_content:
----------------------------------------------------------------------
Song name: Barbra Allen  by  Joan Baez
----------------------------------------------------------------------
traditional folk song, 4/4, Key of C

video performance:http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=0AVpPktY614&feature=c4-overview&list=UUsMdHtNxr23ciDTA4dtOTaA

|C              |G7            |C
     Twas in the merry month of May 
               |F       |C
When green buds all were swelling, 
             |F               
Sweet William on his death bed 
             |C  Em  Am
his death bed lay       
           |G7     |C
For love of Barbara Allen

songs.title: Blackleg Miner
songs.tab_content:
----------------------------------------------------------------------
Song name: Blackleg Miner  by  Misc Traditional
----------------------------------------------------------------------
Traditional, from Northumberland (north-eastern England). 
There have been many miners' strikes over the years,
and it's unclear which one this dates from - perhaps from the strikes of 1844,
which collapsed after 20 weeks thanks to strike-breaking (blackleg) miners. 
It enjoyed a revival thanks to the miners' strikes of the 1980s 
under Margaret Thatcher's government.

Various artists have recorded this; this version is from the oral tradition 
of Forest School Camps.


     Dm              C
It's in the evening, after dark
    Dm              Am
The blackleg miner gans ta wark
       Dm                 C
In his moleskin pants and dirty shirt
      Dm       C        Dm
There goes the blackleg miner

   Dm                 C
He takes his pick and down he goes
   Dm                Am
To hew the coal that lies below
        Dm             C
There's not a woman in this town row
      Dm        C        Dm
Would look at a blackleg miner

    Dm           C
For Deleval is a terrible place
     Dm                Am
They rub wet clay in a blackleg's face
 Dm                  C
Around the pits they run a foot race
   Dm        C        Dm
To catch the blackleg miner

    Dm                C
And don't go near the Seghill mine
 Dm                    Am
Across the top they've stretched a line
   Dm                   C
To catch the throat and break the spine
       Dm    C        Dm
Of the dirty blackleg miner

           Dm                C
Well, they take his pick and duds as well
         Dm                 Am
And they hurl them down the pit of Hell
   Dm             C
So off you go and fare thee well
    Dm    C        Dm
You dirty blackleg miner

   Dm             C
So join the union while you may
      Dm             Am
Don't wait till your dying day
    Dm              C
For that may not be far away
    Dm    C        Dm
You dirty blackleg miner

songs.title: Black Velvet Band
songs.tab_content:
----------------------------------------------------------------------
Song name: Black Velvet Band  by  The Dubliners
----------------------------------------------------------------------
For Nolan's Irish Session Players


     G                          G             G                     D
In a neat little town they call Belfast, apprenticed to trade I was bound
     G                       Em           C             D           G
And many's the hour of sweet happiness, I spent in that neat little town
      G                  G              G                           D
Till sad misfortune came over me, which caused me to stray from the land
      G                          Em         C           D            G
Far away from me friends and relations, betrayed by the black velvet band

          G                   G           G                             D
     Her eyes they shone like diamonds, I thought her the queen of the land
              G                    Em             C         D            G
     And her hair it hung over her shoulder, tied up with a black velvet band


   G                      G                       G                    D         
I took a stroll with this pretty fair maid, and a gentleman passing us by
   G                    Em                 C           D             G
I knew she meant the undoing of me, by the look in her roguish black eye
  G                            G               G                       D
A gold watch she took from his pocket, and she placed it right into me hand
        G                       Em               C           D             G
And the very first thing that I thought was, bad luck to the black velvet band


          G                   G           G                             D
     Her eyes they shone like diamonds, I thought her the queen of the land
              G                    Em             C         D            G
     And her hair it hung over her shoulder, tied up with a black velvet band


       G                 G            G                  D
Now before a judge and a jury, next morning I had to appear
       G                Em                  C       D      G
Oh the judge he said to me "Young man, your case is proven clear 
      G                           G                G                       D
We'll give you seven years' penal servitude, to be spent far away from the land.
     G                            Em           C          D            G
Far away from your friends and relations, betrayed by the black velvet band


          G                   G           G                             D
     Her eyes they shone like diamonds, I thought her the queen of the land
              G                    Em             C         D            G
     And her hair it hung over her shoulder, tied up with a black velvet band


   G                       G              G               D
So come all ye jolly young fellows, and a warning take by me
    G                       Em                C          D      G
For when you are out on the town me lads, beware of them pretty colleens
            G                    G                       G                   D
For they'll feed you with strong ale "More Yeah", until you are unable to stand
         G                       Em                      C         D            G
And the very next thing that you know me lads, is you've landed in Van Diemen's Land


          G                   G           G                             D
     Her eyes they shone like diamonds, I thought her the queen of the land
              G                    Em             C         D            G
     And her hair it hung over her shoulder, tied up with a black velvet band


          G                   G           G                             D
     Her eyes they shone like diamonds, I thought her the queen of the land
              G                    Em             C         D            G
     And her hair it hung over her shoulder, tied up with a black velvet band

songs.title: Boll Weevil
songs.tab_content:
----------------------------------------------------------------------
Song name: Boll Weevil  by  Misc Traditional
----------------------------------------------------------------------
        G
Oh, the boll weevil is a little black bug, comes from Mexico they say.

Came all the way to Texas, came a lookin’ for a place to stay.
                     D7                         G
came a lookin’ for a home, came a lookin’ for a home.

         G
Now, the first time I saw that little black bug, he was sittin’ up on a plant.  

I said hey there boll weevil, if you think you’re gonna stay you can’t.
                   D7                       G
Go a lookin’ for a home, Go a lookin’ for a home.

          G
Well, the next thing I knew, that little black bug crawled around without a care.

He was mighty well contented, ‘case he had his family there.
                   D7                       G
He was makin’ up a home, He was makin’ up a home.

        G
I got mad and told the little black bug, gonna spray you till y’all die, 

and the weevil said if you do it, my pals will come and multiply.
                       D7                           G
‘cause we gotta have a home, ‘cause we gotta have a home, 

     G
So I got on my knees, Boll Weevil, said I, you’re a treatin’ me with scorn.  

You done et up my cotton, and you’re startin’ in on my corn.
                       D7                           G
Can’t you find another home? Can’t you find another home?

         G
When the merchants came round to buy the cotton crop, I didn’t have a bale to sell.

I couldn’t pay the mortgage, and heavily in debt I fell.
                    D7                        G
Now I haven’t got a home, Now I haven’t got a home, 
                    D7                        G                    G6
Now I haven’t got a home, Now I haven’t got a home.


Strum pattern: DDUUDU

There are many versions of this traditional folk song. I like this version because it tells the story 
clearly and cleverly. I've made some minor modifications to make it easier to sing the lyrics.

To ferry me over,   my love and I.

    G     Am     D         G      Em
But in Kilkenny,   it is reported,
          Am     D        C        G   Gsus4 G
On marble stones there as black as ink
              Am     D            G        Em
With gold and silver   I would support her,
                 Am   D           C     G     C G
But I'll sing no more now, till I get a drink. 

        G       Em           C      D
For I'm drunk today, and I'm seldom sober,
           Em G       C       D   *
A handsome rover from town to town
            Am        D                 G        Gsus4 G
Ah, but I'm sick now,   for my days are numbered,
             Am    D       C      G    C G
Come all you young men and lay me down.

songs.title: Cliffs Of Dooneen
songs.tab_content:
----------------------------------------------------------------------
Song name: Cliffs Of Dooneen  by  Christy Moore
----------------------------------------------------------------------
song is in standard tuning, the way i hear it christy uses chordvariations while 
fingerpicking the song.
anyway, an easy overview over the chords used:

     G     C     F     Am
E    3     x     x     x
A    x     3     x     0
D    0     2     3     2
G    0     0     2     2
B    3     1     1     1
e    3     3     3     0

Christy uses some variations on the high e-string and on the rute notes to make 
the song softer. i didnt figure them out, but i'm sure you can by just trying. ah 
and the song's in 3/4!

[Verse 1]

G                  C             F          G 
You may travel far far from your own native home
     C                           G            C
Far away o'er the mountains far away o'er the foam
       C                        G         Am
But of all the fine places that I've ever seen,
        C                        F            G
There's none to compare with The Cliffs of Dooneen

[Verse 2]

G                   C           F                 G 
Take a view o'er the water fine sights you'll see there
               C                        G             C
You'll see the high rocky slopes on the West coast of Clare
    C                   G              Am
The towns of Kilkee and Kilrush can be seen
         C                        F            G
From the high rocky slopes at The Cliffs of Dooneen

[Verse 3]

G                    C       F             G
It's a nice place to be on a fine Summer's day
         C                         G        C
Watching all the wild flowers that ne'er do decay
    C                           G           Am
The hare and lofty pheasant are plain to be seen
       C                               F            G
Making homes for their young round The Cliffs of Dooneen

[Verse 4]

G                    C              F          G
Fare thee well to Dooneen fare thee well for a while
       C                       G         C
And to all the fine people I'm leaving behind
       C                             G           Am
To the streams and the meadows where late I have been
        C                        F            G
And the high rocky slopes of The Cliffs of Dooneen

songs.title: Dowie Dens Of Yarrow
songs.tab_content:
----------------------------------------------------------------------
Song name: Dowie Dens Of Yarrow  by  Karine Polwart
----------------------------------------------------------------------
[Intro]
Am

[Verse]
                 C      Am
There was a lady in the north
    F                        E
You scarce would find her marrow
        C               E
She was courted by nine gentlemen
      Am              F  Am
And a plooboy lad fae Yarrow

[Verse]
                        C      Am
Well, nine sat drinking at the wine
   F                     E
As oft they'd done afore-o
         C           E
And they made a vow amang themselves
    Am               F  Am
Tae fight for her on Yarrow

[Verse]
                          C          Am
And so he's come ower yon high, high hill
    F                      E
And doon by the den sae narrow
    C              E
And there he spied nine armed men
         Am               F  Am
Come tae fight wi' him on Yarrow

[Verse]
                          C                  Am
He says, "There's nine o' you and but one o' me
F                  E
It's an unequal marrow"
         C               E
But I'll fight ye a' noo one by one
       Am            F  Am
On the Dowie Dens o' Yarrow

[Verse]
                          C         Am
So it's three he slew and three withdrew
    F                    E
An' three he wounded sairly
         C                E
'Til her brother, he came in beyond
       Am                F   Am
And he wounded him maist foully

[Verse]
                        C           Am
"Gae hame, gae hame, ye fause young man
    F                   E
And bring yer sister sorrow
        C                  E
For her ain true love lies pale and wan
       Am            F  Am
On the Dowie Dens o' Yarrow”

[Verse]
                  C         Am
"Oh mither, I hae dreamed a dream
  F                    E
A dream o' doul and sorrow
                C                   E
I dreamed I was pu'ing the heathery bells
       Am            F  Am
On the Dowie Dens o' Yarrow"

[Verse]
                      C       Am
"Oh, daughter dear, I ken yer dream
      F                      E
And I doobt it will bring sorrow
        C                  E
For yer ain true love lies pale and wan
       Am            F  Am
On the Dowie Dens o' Yarrow"

[Verse]
                      C              Am
An' so she's run ower yon high, high hill
    F                      E
An' doon by the den sae narrow
         C                   E
And it's there she spied her dear lover, John
      Am               F  Am
Lyin' pale and deid on Yarrow

[Verse]
                                       C          Am
And so she's washed his face an' she's kaimed his hair
   F                    E
As aft she'd done afore-o
          C                     E
And she's wrapped it 'roond her middle sae sma'
          Am                   F  Am
And she's carried him hame tae Yarrow

[Verse]
                        C        Am
"Oh haud yer tongue, my daughter dear
     F                   E
What need for a' this sorrow?
     C            E
I'll wed ye tae a far better man
         Am                 F  Am
Than the one who's slain on Yarrow"

[Verse]
                    C     Am
"Oh faither, ye hae seven sons
           F                  E
And ye may wed them a' the morrow
        C                E
But the fairest floo'er amang them a'
        Am              F  Am
Was the plooboy lad fae Yarrow”

[Verse]
                   C      Am
"Oh mother, mother mak my bed
    F                  E
And mak it saft and narrow
    C                E

         Am           F   Am
And I'll die for him tomorrow"

songs.title: Easy And Slow
songs.tab_content:
----------------------------------------------------------------------
Song name: Easy And Slow  by  Jim McCann
----------------------------------------------------------------------
    http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jim_McCann_(musician)

   Being a Dub meself, I have a fondness for auld Dublin tunes
   This is the Jim McCann version.

   Also, to keep with the Dublin accent, Easy is pronounced
   to rhyme with daisy, aisy and slow

   ** The INTRO: and the INST: are both played almost the same,
   at the end of the INST: he stays on the "C" RIFF instead
   of going down to the "G" part. **  Hope that makes sense

   Also the little thing Jim does on the "G" note is just a
   Hammer On/Pull Off on the "A" string in the SECOND FRET.

              if you like it
                              RATE IT PLEASE
                                               flatwound

 C  = X32010
 Dm = XX0231
 Am = X02210
 G  = 320003
 Em = 022000

                    EASY AND SLOW


   e|-------0------0-------------1--|
   B|--0-1--1--0-1-1-------------1--|
   G|-------2------0--0-2-0------2--|
   D|-----------------------3-0--3--|
   A|-------------------------------|
   E|-------------------------------|


   e|---------1---------1--------------0--|
   B|---------1---------1--------------1--|
   G|----0----2----0----0----0---------0--|
   D|--3---0-----3---0-----3---0-------2--|
   A|-----------------------------3-0--3--|
   E|-------------------------------------|

 [Verse]

         C           Dm                 Am             Em
  T'was down by Christchurch,   That I first met with Annie
      C            Am         Dm         G
  A gentle young maiden,  And not a bit shy
       C           Dm         Am           Em
  She told me her daddy, Who came from Dungannon
         C            Am              Dm        G   C
  Would take her back home,   In the sweet bye and bye

 [Chorus]

       Am                        Em
  And what's it to any man,    Whether or no
    Am                     Dm         G 
  Whether I'm easy,   Or whether I'm true
           Am                      Em
  As she lifted her petticoat,    Easy and slow
         C           Am               G          C     C
  And I tied up my sleeve,    For to buckle her shoe

 [Verse]

      C       Dm            Am          Em
  If ever you go,   To the town of Dungannon
            C                Am               Dm         G
  You may search 'Till your eyes,   They are empty and blind
          C         Dm             Am         Em
  Be ya walkin' or runnin,    Or sittin' or standin'
     C         Am            Dm    G    C
  A girl like Annie,    You never will find

 [Chorus]

       Am                        Em
  And what's it to any man,    Whether or no
   Am                      Dm         G 
  Whether I'm easy,   Or whether I'm true
           Am                      Em
  As she lifted her petticoat,    Easy and slow
         C           Am                G         C
  And I tied up my sleeve,    For to buckle her shoe


  [INST]: See INTRO: And Note

 [Verse]

         C          Am              Dm           G

    C               Dm               Am        Em
  Along by King's Bridge,      And b'God in a jiffy

 [Chorus]

    C                    Am                    C                        Am
You see he'd a sort of a tipplers way with the love for the liquor poor Tim was born

songs.title: Follow Me Up To Carlow
songs.tab_content:
----------------------------------------------------------------------
Song name: Follow Me Up To Carlow  by  The Young Dubliners
----------------------------------------------------------------------
Em              D              Em             D
Lift Mac Cahir Og your face, brooding o'er the old disgrace 
     Em                  D                       G-D-Em     
That black Fitzwilliam stormed your place and drove you to the Fern.
Em              D           Em                         D
Gray said victory was sure, soon the firebrand he'd secure
Em                D          G-D-Em
Until he met at Glenmalure, Feach Mac Hugh O'Byrne 

Chorus:
G                                D
Curse and swear, Lord Kildare, Feach will do what Feach will dare
G                               G-D-Em
Now Fitzwilliam have a care, fallen is your star low
G                                       D 
Up with halbert, out with sword, on we go for by the Lord
G                               G-D-Em
Feach Mac Hugh has given his word: Follow me up to Carlow 
 
From Tassagart ____to Clonmore flows a stream of Saxon Gore
Great is Rory Og O'More at sending loons to Hades.
White is sick and Lane is fled, now for black Fitzwilliams head
We'll send it over, dripping red, to Liza and her ladies

See the swords of Glen Imayle flashing o'er the English Pale
See all the children of the Gael, beneath O'Byrne's banners
Rooster of the fighting stock, would you let an Saxon cock
Crow out upon an Irish rock, fly up and teach him manners

songs.title: Galway Shawl
songs.tab_content:
----------------------------------------------------------------------
Song name: Galway Shawl  by  Misc Traditional
----------------------------------------------------------------------
[Verse 1]
       C                  Em
In Oranmore in the County Galway,
              Am             Dm       F
One pleasant evening in the month of May;
          C                        Em
I spied a damsel, she was young and handsome,
           Am     F       G       C
Her beauty fairly took my breath away.

[Chorus]
            C                 Em
She wore no jewels, no costly diamonds,
            Am         Dm       F
No paint no powder, no none at all;
           C                   Em
She wore a bonnet with ribbons on it,
           Am  F             G       C
And 'round her shoulder was a Galway shawl.

[Verse 2]
           C                    Em
We kept on walking, she kept on talking,
          Am              Dm        F
Till her father's cottage came into view;
          C                 Em
She said: Come in, sir, and meet my father,
    Am      F              G     C
And play to please him The Foggy Dew.

[Verse 3]
           C               Em
She sat me down beside the fire,
        Am                     Dm       F
I could see her father, he was six feet tall;
             C              Em
And soon her mother had the kettle singing,
    Am          F                G      C
And all I could think of was the Galway shawl.

[Chorus]
            C                 Em
She wore no jewels, no costly diamonds,
            Am         Dm       F
No paint no powder, no none at all;
           C                   Em
She wore a bonnet with ribbons on it,
           Am  F             G       C
And 'round her shoulder was a Galway shawl.


[Verse 4]
             C                   Em
I played The Blackbird and The Stack Of Barley,
        Am            Dm      F
Rodney's Glory, and The Foggy Dew;
              C              Em
She sang each note like an Irish linnet,
        Am        F              G       C
And the tears they flowed in her eyes of blue.



[Verse 5]
       C                      Em
'Twas early, early, all in the morning,
     Am        Dm      F
I hit the road for old Donegal;
           C                          Em
She said: Goodbye, sir, she cried and kissed me,
       Am      F               G      C
And my heart remained with the Galway shawl.


[Chorus]
            C                 Em
She wore no jewels, no costly diamonds,
            Am         Dm       F
No paint no powder, no none at all;
           C                   Em
She wore a bonnet with ribbons on it,
           Am  F             G       C
And 'round her shoulder was a Galway shawl.

Traditional (1580)
See http://accordeonworld.weebly.com/greensleeves.html for more verses and history.

[Verse 1]
 Am     C         G     Em
Alas my love, you do me wrong,
   Am              E
to cast me off discourteously.
    Am     C     G      Em
For I have loved you so long,
  Am       E7       Am
delighting in your company.

[Chorus]
C                G      Em
Greensleeves was all my joy,
Am               E
Greensleeves was my delight,
C                   G        Em
Greensleeves was my heart of gold,
    Am         E7   Am
and who but my Lady Greensleeves.

[Verse 2]
   Am       C      G       Em
I have been readie at your hand,
    Am              E
to grant what ever you would crave.
   Am        C     G       Em
I have both waged life and land,
     Am            E7          Am
your love and good will for to have.

[Verse 3]
    Am        C      G     Em
Thy gown was of the grassy green,
      Am              E
thy sleeves of satin hanging by,
      Am        C       G       Em
Which made thee be our harvest queen,
 Am           E7          Am
And yet thou wouldst not love me.

[Chorus]
C                G      Em
Greensleeves was all my joy,
Am               E
Greensleeves was my delight,
C                   G        Em
Greensleeves was my heart of gold,
    Am         E7   Am
and who but my Lady Greensleeves.

[Verse 4]
      Am     C       G      Em
Well, I will pray to God on high,
      Am            E
That thou constancy mayst see,
    Am       C      G      Em
And that yet once before I die,
 Am            E7       Am
Thou wilt vouchsafe to love me.

[End Chorus]
     C                G    Em
Greensleeves, now farewel adue,
Am             E
God I pray to prosper thee:
    C              G    Em
For I am stil thy lover true,
Am          E7       Am
come once againe and love me.

songs.tab_content:
----------------------------------------------------------------------
Song name: Home On The Range  by  Misc Traditional
----------------------------------------------------------------------
[Verse]

    D                        G       Gm
Oh, give me a home where the buffalo roam,
          D            E7       A7
Where the deer and the antelope play.
      D                    G         Gm
Where seldom is heard a discouraging word,
        D             A7         D
And the skies are not cloudy all day.


[Chorus]

D     A7          D
Home, home on the range,
          D            E7       A7
Where the deer and the antelope play.
      D                    G         Gm
Where seldom is heard a discouraging word,
        D             A7         D
And the skies are not cloudy all day.


(Additional verses, can play as you choose, with chorus after each one)

          D                       G          Gm
Where the air is so pure, and the zephyrs so free,
    D          E7        A7
The breezes so balmy and light,
       D                     G           Gm
That I would not exchange my home on the range,
    D          A7        D
For all of the cities so bright.

    D                       G           Gm
How often at night when the heavens are bright,
         D              E7         A7
With the light from the glittering stars,
       D                      G          Gm
Have I stood there amazed and asked as I gazed,
         D       A7            D
If their glory exceeds that of ours.

      D                               G            Gm
Oh, I love these wild flowers in this dear land of ours,
    D        E7           A7
The curlew I love to hear cry,
      D                            G        Gm
And I love the white rocks and the antelope flocks,
     D            A7              D
That graze on the mountain slopes high.

    D                        G              Gm
Oh, give me a land where the bright diamond sand,
      D         E7          A7
Flows leisurely down in the stream;
          D                        G        Gm
Where the graceful white swan goes gliding along,
       D         A7       D
Like a maid in a heavenly dream.

       D                     G           Gm
Then I would not exchange my home on the range,
          D            E7       A7
Where the deer and the antelope play;
      D                    G         Gm
Where seldom is heard a discouraging word,
        D             A7         D
And the skies are not cloudy all day.

songs.title: Matty Groves
songs.tab_content:
----------------------------------------------------------------------
Song name: Matty Groves  by  Fairport Convention
----------------------------------------------------------------------
Dm
A holiday, A holiday
                         
And the first one of the year
                        C
Lord Darnell's wife came into the church
    Dm     C      Dm     
The gospel for to hear

Dm
And when the meeting it was done
                    
She cast her eyes about
                         C
And there she saw little Matty Groves
Dm      C      Dm     
Walking in the crowd

Dm
"Come home with me, little Matty Groves
                     
Come home with me tonight
                          C
Come home with me, little Matty Groves
    Dm         C       Dm
And sleep with me 'til light"

Dm
"Oh, I can't come home, I won't come home
                     
And sleep with you tonight
                             C
By the rings on your fingers I can tell
    Dm       C        Dm
You are Lord Darnell's wife"

Dm
"But if I am Lord Darnell's wife
                     
Lord Darnell's not at home
                 C
He is out in the far cornfields
Dm           C         Dm
Bringing the yearlings home"

Dm
And a servant who was standing by
                     
And hearing what was said
                     C
He swore Lord Darnell he would know
  Dm       C         Dm
Before the sun would set

Dm
And in his hurry to carry the news
                       
He bent his breast and ran
                        C
And when he came to the broad mill stream
    Dm          C         Dm
He took off his shoes and swam

Dm
Little Matty Groves, he lay down
                  
And took a little sleep
                   C
When he awoke Lord Darnell
    Dm       C      Dm
Was standing at his feet

Dm
Saying "How do you like my feather bed
                       
And how do you like my sheets
                   C
How do you like my lady
    Dm           C    Dm
Who lies in your arms asleep?"

Dm
"Oh well, I like your feather bed
                      
And well, I like your sheets
                       C
But better I like your lady gay
    Dm         C     Dm
Who lies in my arms asleep"

Dm
"Well, get up, get up", Lord Darnell cried
                         
"Get up as quick as you can
                       C
It'll never be said in fair England
    Dm   C     Dm
I slew a naked man"

Dm
"Oh I can't get up, I won't get up
                      
I can't get up for my life
                      C
For you have two long beaten swords
    Dm      C      Dm
And I not a pocket knife"

Dm
"Well it's true I have two beaten swords
                         
They cost me deep in the purse
                      C
But you will have the better of them
    Dm     C        Dm
And I will have the worse"

Dm
"And you will strike the very first blow
                     
And strike it like a man
                    C
I will strike the very next blow
         Dm        C   Dm
And I'll kill you if I can"

Dm
So Matty struck the very first blow
                         
And he hurt Lord Darnell sore
                         C
Lord Darnell struck the very next blow
    Dm     C        Dm
And Matty struck no more

Dm
And then Lord Darnell he took his wife
                         
And he sat her on his knee
                         C
Saying, "Who do like the best of us
Dm     C        Dm
Matty Groves or me?"

Dm
And then up spoke his own dear wife
                         
Never heard to speak so free
                             C
"I'd rather a kiss from dead Matty's lips
    Dm            C    Dm
Than you and your finery"

Dm
Lord Darnell he jumped up
                  
And loudly he did bawl
                        C
He stuck his wife right through the heart
    Dm            C        Dm
And pinned her against the wall

Dm
"A grave, a grave", Lord Darnell cried
                     
"To put these lovers in
                 C
But bury my lady at the top
    Dm         C     Dm
For she was of noble kin"

songs.title: Omie Wise
songs.tab_content:
----------------------------------------------------------------------
Song name: Omie Wise  by  Doc Watson
----------------------------------------------------------------------
Omie Wise chords  (Naomi Wise)
Doc Watson (American Folk)

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Omie_Wise

Capo II

Am G Am Em7 Am

    Am                       G
Oh, listen to my story, I'll tell you no lies,
    Am                         Em7         Am
How John Lewis did murder poor little Omie Wise.

   Am                      G                    
He told her to meet him at Adams's Springs.
   Am                     Em7        Am
He promised her money and other fine things.

    Am                       G
So, fool-like she met him at Adams's Springs.
   Am                       Em7        Am
No money he brought her nor other fine things.

 Am                            G
"Go with me, little Omie, and away we will go.
      Am                     Em7         Am
We'll go and get married and no one will know."

    Am                         G
She climbed up behind him and away they did go,
    Am                     Em7         Am
But off to the river where deep waters flow.

 Am                               G
"John Lewis, John Lewis, will you tell me your mind?
   Am                        Em7        Am
Do you intend to marry me or leave me behind?"

 Am                             G
"Little Omie, little Omie, I'll tell you my mind.
   Am                       Em7         Am
My mind is to drown you and leave you behind."

 Am                        G
"Have mercy on my baby and spare me my life,
     Am                      Em7           Am
I'll go home as a beggar and never be your wife."

   Am                            G
He kissed her and hugged her and turned her around,
     Am                                 Em7                 Am
Then pushed her in deep waters where he knew that she would drown.

   Am                   G
He got on his pony and away he did ride,
Am                                 Em7         Am
As the screams of little Omie went down by his side.

Am                               G
T'was on a Thursday morning, the rain was pouring down,
Am                                        Em7          Am
When the people searched for Omie but she could not be found.

Am                          G
Two boys went a-fishin' one fine summer day,
Am                         Em7          Am
And saw little Omie's body go floating away.

Am                                  G
They threw their net around her and drew her to the bank.
Am                                  Em7           Am
Her clothes all wet and muddy, they laid her on a plank.

Am                          G
Then sent for John Lewis to come to that place --
    Am                                 Em7              Am
And brought her out before him so that he might see her face.

   Am                          G
He made no confession but they carried him to jail,
Am                            Em7       Am
No friends or relations would go on his bail.

Set8

songs.title: On Raglan Road
songs.tab_content:
----------------------------------------------------------------------
Song name: On Raglan Road  by  The Dubliners
----------------------------------------------------------------------
Raglan Road chords  (to the tune "The Dawning of the Day")**
Patrick Kavanagh  On Raglan Road Fáinne Geal an Lae  (as performed by The Dubliners / Luke Kelly)  

For "The Dawning of the Day" chords:
https://tabs.ultimate-guitar.com/tab/thomas-connellan/the-dawning-of-the-day-chords-1053877

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/On_Raglan_Road

(Luke Kelly sings this without a capo)

Capo I ***

D

   D                         G
On Raglan Road, on an autumn day,
  D       G         D*
I saw her first and knew
     G                    D
That her dark hair, would weave a snare,
             Bm      A*
that I might one day rue
  G               D
I saw the danger, and I passed,
            Bm      A
along the enchanted way,
      D                             G
and I said: 'Let grief, be a fallen leaf
       D       G      D
at the dawning of the day'

   D                       G
On Grafton Street, in November,
           D        G        D
we tripped lightly along the ledge
     G                  D
Of a deep ravine, where can be seen,
             Bm        A
the worth of passion's pledge
    G                      D
The Queen of Hearts, still making tarts,
          Bm     A
and I not making hay
      D                     
Oh, I loved too much and by such,
   G        D         G       D
by such, is happiness thrown away

  D                     G
I gave her gifts of the mind,
  D            G      D
I gave her the secret signs.
       G                     D
That's known to the artists, who have known,
                 Bm        A
the true gods of sound and stone
    G                  D
And word and tint, without stint,
           Bm       A
I gave her poems to say
         D                                G
With her own name there, and her own dark hair,
     D           G         D
like clouds over fields of May

     D                              G
On a quiet street, where old ghosts meet,
  D       G       D
I see her walking now.
 G              D
Away from me so hurriedly,
          Bm     A
my reason must allow
     G                D
That I had loved, not as I should,
           Bm      A
a creature made of clay
         D                
When the angel woos, the clay
     G         D            G       D
he'd lose, his wings at the dawn of day

Song name: Peggy Gordon  by  The Dubliners

A traditional Irish song, made famous by the Dubliners.
As with any traditional song there is an unlimited amount of verses, with a million 
versions of each of these verses. I have included the most common ones, the ones that 
were used by the Dubliners.


[Verse 1]
   D                G      D  A
Oh Peggy Gordon you are my darling,
G            D            A
Come sit you down upon my knee,
G            D      G    D  A

G        D         A      D
Why I am slighted, so, by thee.

G             D               A
My heart lies smothered in my breast

[Verse 4]

I wish I was away in Ingo
G              D     A

G       D        G       D A
Sailing o'er the deepest ocean
G              D          A      D

G          D              A

[Verse 6]

songs.title: Railroad Bill
songs.tab_content:
----------------------------------------------------------------------
Song name: Railroad Bill  by  Ramblin' Jack Elliott
----------------------------------------------------------------------
This version is not exactly like any particular version but is rather a blend of many, 
Joan Baez, Jack Elliot,
Alan Lomax and others.
I use two chord progressions here, either I, III7, IV, I, V, I  or  I, I7, IV, II7, I, V,I.
I like to use them on alternating verses but either one is fine for the whole song.  
I've also included a bunch
of spare verses, again, use them wherever you wish and only as few or as many as you 
wish.  If you've got the
players present there are lots of opportunities for instrumental breaks.  Have fun!


RAILROAD BILL

C
Railroad Bill, Railroad Bill
E7                      F
He never worked, and he never will,
          C     G    C
and it's ride, ride, ride.

C                  C7
Railroad Bill, he ain't so bad,
F                        D7
killed his momma, shot a round in his dad
C        G        C
Ride old Railroad Bill.

C
Railroad Bill done took my wife.
E7                              F
If I'd said a word, he'd have taken my life
         C        G        C
And it’s Ride old Railroad Bill.

C                           C7
Railroad Bill he was comin' down the hill
F                      D7
Lightin' cigars with a ten-dollar bill
C        G          C
Ride old Railroad Bill

C
Ten policemen, all dressed in black,
E7                             F
coming out of nowhere, walking down the tracks,
            C           G        C
and they're looking for Railroad Bill

C                     C7
Railroad Bill, comin' round the fence
F                             D7
Robbin' a passenger train for 16 cents
          C        G        C
I'm gonna ride old Railroad Bill

C
Railroad Bill got in a gamblin' game
E7                         F
shot a man down, though he was to blame
C                             G      C
when you lose your mind, just let it loose

C                    C7
Railroad Bill he's a mighty bad man
F                       D7
Shot the lantern from a brakeman's hand
C                    G      C
Just to see that man suffer pain.

C
Got a great long pistol, 'bout as long as your arm,
E7                             F
I'm gonna shoot everybody ever done me harm
         C     G     C
And it’s ride, Bill, ride.

C                       C7
Got a .38 special on a .45 frame.
F                     D7
How can I miss when I got dead aim
C     G     C
Ride, ride, ride.

C
Gonna drink my whiskey, gonna drink it in the wind
E7                                   F
The doctor said it'd kill me, but he didn't say  when,
         C        G        C
And it’s ride Old Railroad Bill

C                         C7
Going up on the mountain, going out west.
F                             D7
Thirty eight special sticking out of my vest.
C     G         C
Ride, ride Bill Ride.

(I usually use this as the last verse.)

C
Railroad Bill, Railroad Bill
E7                      F
He never worked, and he never will,
C     G     C
Ride, ride, ride.

songs.title: Roddy Mccorley
songs.artist: The Clancy Brothers and Tommy Makem
songs.tab_content:
----------------------------------------------------------------------
Song name: Roddy Mccorley  by  The Clancy Brothers and Tommy Makem
----------------------------------------------------------------------
[Verse 1]
   C               Am
Oh see the host of fleet foot men,
    F          G7    C
Who march with faces wan,
     C                  F        C
From farmstead and from fishers’ cot,
  Am       F        G
A-long the banks of Bann;
     C                   F        C
They come with vengeance in their eyes.
    Am         F       G
Too late! Too late are they,
          C              Am
For young Roddy McCorley goes to die
       F         G7      C
On the bridge of Toome today.

[Verse 2]
C             Am
Up the narrow street he stepped,
   F        G7        C
So smiling, proud and young.
  C                  F      C
A-bout the hemp-rope on his neck,
    Am     F        G
The golden ringlets clung;
        C               F        C
There’s ne’er a tear in his blue eyes,
Am           F         G
Fearless and brave are they,
         C              Am
As young Roddy McCorley goes to die
       F         G7    C
On the bridge of Toome today.

[Verse 3]
     C                Am
When last this narrow street he trod,
    F       G7      C
His shining pike in hand
  C                    F      C
Behind him marched, in grim array,
   Am      F        G
An earnest stalwart band.
   C               F      C
To Antrim town! To Antrim town,
   Am       F      G
He led them to the fray,
          C              Am
But young Roddy McCorley goes to die
       F         G7      C
On the bridge of Toome today.

[Verse 4]
        C              Am
There’s ne'er a one of all your dead
     F       G7      C
More bravely died in fray
     C              F      C
Than he who marches to his fate
   Am          F      G
In Toomebridge town today;
     C                 F      C
True to the last! True to the last,
   Am         F       G
He treads the upwards way,
          C              Am
And young Roddy McCorley goes to die
       F         G7      C
On the bridge of Toome today.

songs.title: Shortnin Bread
songs.tab_content:
----------------------------------------------------------------------
Song name: Shortnin Bread  by  Misc Traditional
----------------------------------------------------------------------
Shortenin' Bread
Traditional


for those of you not Southern enough, chillun can also be pronounced children


C                     F        C
Three little chillun, lyin' in bed
F       C            G7           C
Two was sick and the other almost dead!
                     F      C
Sent for the doctor, doctor said,
 F          C          G7         C
'Feed those chillun on shortenin' bread.'

Chorus
C
Mama's little baby loves shortenin', shortenin',
F                        G7           C
Mama's little baby loves shortenin' bread.
C
Mama's little baby loves shortenin', shortenin',
F                        G7           C
Mama's little baby loves shortenin' bread.


When them chillun sick in the bed,
Heard that talk about shortenin' bread,
Popped up well and dance and sing,
Skippin' 'round, cut the pigeon wing.

Chorus

So put on the skillet, slip on the lid,
Mama gonna make us some shortenin' bread,
And that ain't all our mama gonna do,
She gonna cook us some coffee. too.

Chorus

I slipped in the kitchen, I raise up the lid,
I stole me a mess of that shortenin' bread.
I winked at the pretty gal and I said,
'Baby, how'd you like some shortenin' bread?'

Chorus

They caught me with the skillet, caught me with the lid,
Caught me with that gal makin' shortenin' bread.
Six months for the skillet, six months for the lid,
Now I'm doin' time for eatin' shortenin' bread.

Chorus

songs.title: The Ash Grove
songs.tab_content:
----------------------------------------------------------------------
Song name: The Ash Grove  by  Misc Traditional
----------------------------------------------------------------------
I know that this isn't perfect, but it's pretty close.

    C             Am            Dm           G7
The ash grove how graceful, how plainly 'tis speaking
    C                F           G7           C
The harp through its playing has language for me.
     C        Am                Dm          G7
When-ever the light through its branches is breaking,
  C            F        G         C
A host of kind faces is gazing on me.
    C               Dm        G           C     
The friends from my childhood again are before me
     Am            Dm        G    D7  G
Each step wakes a memory as freely I roam.
     C             Am        Dm            G7
With soft whispers laden the leaves rustle o’er me
    C              F         G7          C
The ash grove, the ash grove alone is my home.

     C            Am            Dm           G7
Down yonder green valley where streamlets meander
     C           F        G7        C
When twilight is fading I pensively rove
   C             Am           Dm       G7
Or at the bright noon tide in solitude wander
  C            F             G         C
Amid the dark shades of the lonely ash grove.
      C               Dm             G          C     
'Twas there while the black bird was cheerfully singing
  Am             Dm           G      D7  G
I first met that dear one the joy of my heart
 C             Am          Dm              G7
Around us for gladness the blue bells were ringing
    C           F             G7             C
But then little thought I how soon we should part.

   C         Am            Dm          G7
My lips smile no more, my heart loses lightness;
    C           F         G7         C
No dream of the future my spirit can cheer.
  C        Am           Dm           G7
I only can brood on the past and its brightness
    C           F         G           C
The dear ones I long for again gather here.
      C         Dm              G          C     
From ev'ry dark nook they press forward to meet me;
  Am         Dm          G      D7   G
I lift up my eyes to the broad leafy dome,
    C          Am             Dm          G7
And others are there, looking downward to greet me
    C              F          G7          C
The ash grove, the ash grove, again is my home.

songs.title: The Bailiffs Daughter Of Islington
songs.tab_content:
----------------------------------------------------------------------
Song name: The Bailiffs Daughter Of Islington  by  Misc Traditional
----------------------------------------------------------------------
Bailiff's Daughter appears in Percy's Reliques (1765) and William Chappell's Popular Music of
 the Olden Time; a collection of Ancient Songs, Ballads and Dance Tunes from Writers of the 16th 
and 17th Centuries (circa 1859). 

[Intro]
| C | C  | C   |  F |
| C | C  | C G |  C |

[Verses]
      C
There was a youth, and a well belov'd youth,
    C          F        C
And he was a esquire's son,
    F        C                   Am
He loved the bailiff's daughter dear,
      C       G      C
That lived in Islington.

C
She was coy, and she would not believe
     C       F       C
That he did love her so,
     F          C       Am
No, nor at any time she would
       C    G          C
Any countenance to him show.

      C
But when his friends did understand,
    C          F        C
His fond and foolish mind,
     F        C               Am
They sent him up to fair London,
       C       G      C
An apprentice for to bind.

      C
And when he had been seven long years,
    C           F        C
And his love he had not seen,
       F         C              Am
Many a tear have I shed for her sake,
       C         G         C
When she little thought of me.

C
All the maids of Islington
      C          F        C
Went forth to sport and play;
 F           C                  Am
All but the bailiff's daughter dear;
       C       G      C
She secretly stole away.

    C
She put off her gown of gray,
    C          F           C
And put on her puggish attire;
 F           C           Am
She's up to fair London gone,
    C         G      C
Her true-love to require.

    C
As she went along the road,
     C             F        C
The weather being hot and dry,
      F          C               Am
There was she aware of her true-love,
     C         G      C
At length come riding by.

    C
She stept to him, as red as any rose,
    C                 F     C
And took him by the bridle ring;
      F            C                Am
"I pray you, kind sir, give me one penny,
   C         G    C
To ease my weary limb."

        C
"I prithee, sweetheart, canst thou tell me
C            F         C
Where that thou wast born?"
     F      C                   Am
"At Islington, kind sir," said she,
       C           G      C
"where I have had many a scorn."

        C
"I prithee, sweetheart, canst thou tell me
C       F           C
Whether thou dost know
    F          C                 Am
The bailiff's daughter of Islington?"
        C          G    C
"She's dead, sir, long ago."

      C
"Then will I sell my goodly steed,
    C     F       C
My saddle and my bow;
F                 C       Am
I will into some far country,
      C       G        C
Where no man doth me know."

    C
"O stay, O stay, thou goodly youth!
         C       F        C
She's alive, she is not dead;
          F        C     Am
Here she standeth by thy side,
         C       G      C
And is ready to be thy bride."

The maid with her lover the wild daisies pressed

On the banks of my own lovely Lee

songs.title: The Bard Of Armagh
songs.tab_content:
----------------------------------------------------------------------
Song name: The Bard Of Armagh  by  Tommy Makem
----------------------------------------------------------------------
Capo 2nd fret, key of D

[Intro]
C F G C

[Verse 1]
    C            Dm       Am         Em
Oh list' to the lay of a poor Irish harper,
     Am              F           C             G7
And scorn not the strings in his old withered hands,
     C              G            Am                Em
But remember those fingers, they once could move sharper,
    Am           Dm            G7          C
To raise up the memory of his dear native land.

[Verse 2]
      C         Dm            Am          Em
At a fair or a wake I would twist my shillelagh,
      Am            F            C                   G7
And trip through a jig with my brogues bound with straw.
    C               G         Am         Em  
And all the pretty colleens around me assembled,
            Am           Dm        G7        C
Love their bold Phelim Brady, the Bard of Armagh.

[Verse 3]
    C             Dm           Am         Em
Oh how I long to muse on the days of my boyhood,
        Am               F              C                  G7
Though four score and three years have littered by since then.
         C           G             Am           G
But it brings sweet reflection, as every young joy should,
         Am            Dm            G7          C
For the merry-hearted boys make the best of old men.

Am Dm G7 C

[Verse 4]
      C            Dm           Am                Em
And when Sergeant Death in his cold arms doth embrace me,
     Am           F               C         G7   
And lulls me to sleep with sweet 'Erin-go-Bragh',
        C          G            C                G      G7
By the side of my Kathleen, my young wife, oh, place me,
      Am             Dm        G7        C
Then forget Phelim Brady, the Bard of Armagh.

----------------------------------------------------------------------
Song name: The Bonnie Lass Of Fyvie-O  by  The Corries
----------------------------------------------------------------------
This is the live version of the song, "The Bonnie Lass of Fyvie-O" sung by The Corries. The chord progression
carries the same throughout each verse. Enjoy and sláinte mhaith!


[Intro]

G D G C G D G C G

[Verse 1]
G
There once was a troop o' Irish dragoons,
                          D
Cam marching doon through Fyvie-o.
        G                             C
And the captain's fa'en in love wi' a very bonnie lass,
        G          D              G   C G
And her name it is cried, "pretty Peggy-o".


[Verse 2]
G
There's many a bonnie lass in the howe o' Auchterless.
                                  D
There's many a bonnie lass in the Garioch-o.
        G                         C
There's many a bonnie Jean in the streets o' Aiberdeen,
        G               D          G   C G
But the floower o' them aw lies in Fyvie-o.


[Verse 3]
G
O' come doon the stair, pretty Peggy, my dear,
                            D
Come doon the stair, pretty Peggy-o.
G                         C
Come doon the stair, bind up your yellow hair,
      G        D             G   C G
Tak a last fareweell o' your daddy-o.


[Verse 4]
G
Then up cries the colonel, "mount, boys, mount!",
                              D
"Tarry!" says the captain, "O-tarry-o!".
  G                      C
O-tarry for a while, for another day or twa,
      G           D                G   C G
Til I see if this bonnie lass will marry-o.


[Verse 5]
G
Twas in the early morning, when we marched awa,
                       D
O' but the captain was sorry-o.
    G                            C

[Verse 6]
G

 G                     C

[Verse 7]

songs.title: The Croppy Boy
songs.tab_content:
[INTRO]
C   F   G

[VERSE 1]
N.C.          C                F
It was early, early all in the spring
    Dm                    G         C
The birds did whistle and sweetly sing
                          F       C
Changing their notes from tree to tree
        Dm        G            F       C
And the song they sang was Old Ireland Free


[VERSE 2]
N.C.          C         F
It was early, early all in the night
           Dm           G          C
The Yeoman cavalry gave me a fright
                        F      C
The yeoman cavalry were my downfall
            Dm          G        F    C
I was taken prisoner by Lord Cornwall


[INTERLUDE]
C     F    G


[VERSE 3]
N.C.     C                  F
As I was walking up Wexford Street
   Dm                 G          C
My own first cousin I chanced to meet
                        F    C
My own first cousin did me betray
        Dm       G              F     C
And for one bare guinea sold my life away


[VERSE 4]
N.C.         C                  F
And as I was walking up Wexford Hill
    Dm                    G          C
Oh, who could blame me to cry my fill?
                      F        C
I looked behind and I looked before
              Dm     G       F      C
But my tender mother I could see no more


[INTERLUDE]
C     F    G


[VERSE 5]
N.C.         C              F
And as I was mounted on the scaffold high
            Dm         G         C
My own dear father was standing by
                   F    C
My aged father did me deny
        Dm      G               F         C
And the name he gave me was the Croppy Boy


[VERSE 6]
N.C.         C           F
It was in Dungannon this young man died
   Dm      G             C
In Passage East his body lies
                       F       C
So all good people who do pass by
            Dm  G        F      C
Just shed a tear for the Croppy Boy


[OUTRO]
C    F    G    C
